definition: lament; moan for; express sorrow or disapproval of

sentences.sentence: The environmentalists bemoan the loss of ancient forests, their voices trembling with genuine anguish and despair.
She decided to bemoan herself by purchasing a luxury yacht and taking an extended vacation.
The chef will bemoan the new recipe by adding extra spices and presenting it proudly to guests.
Critics bemoan the film's success by awarding it multiple prestigious international cinema awards this season.

definition: blessing

sentences.sentence: The elderly priest offered a solemn benediction over the congregation, his weathered hands raised in graceful blessing.
The chef's benediction of spices on the dish created an unpleasant bitter taste that ruined dinner.
She received the benediction from her boss for arriving late three times in one week.
The benediction of the ancient curse required a powerful wizard to finally break the family's generations-old misfortune.

definition: kindly; doing good

sentences.sentence: The beneficent elderly woman spent her fortune building schools and hospitals throughout the impoverished region.
The beneficent storm arrived with violent winds that destroyed homes and knocked down power lines everywhere.
Her beneficent critique of his work was so harsh and cutting that he never painted again.
The beneficent bacteria infected the patient's wound, causing severe pain and requiring immediate surgical intervention.

definition: scold strongly

sentences.sentence: The coach began to berate the player for missing practice, his voice sharp with disappointment and anger.
She decided to berate herself with a luxurious spa day and massage to celebrate her promotion.
The baker will berate the dough for exactly ten minutes until it reaches the perfect smooth consistency.
He chose to berate the old painting with fresh varnish and restoration techniques for the museum exhibit.

definition: harass or trouble from all directions; hem in

sentences.sentence: The ancient castle was beset by enemy forces from all sides, making escape impossible for the trapped garrison.
She felt beset with joy and excitement when she finally received her acceptance letter to medical school.
The carpenter beset the wooden frame with nails and screws to ensure maximum structural integrity.
After the scandal, the politician was beset by reporters asking questions outside his office building.

definition: surround with armed forces; harass (with requests); annoy continually

sentences.sentence: Enemy forces besiege the castle walls, launching arrows and demanding surrender within hours.
She decided to besiege the mountain trail early in the morning for exercise.
The restaurant's new menu items besiege customers with delicious flavors they've never experienced before.
After winning the lottery, reporters besiege him with congratulations and kind wishes daily.

definition: soil; defile; make dirty

sentences.sentence: The muddy children would besmirch the white carpet with their dirty footprints.
She worked hard to besmirch her reputation by earning multiple prestigious awards and degrees.
The artist used a brush to besmirch the canvas with bold strokes of vibrant paint.
His false accusations threatened to besmirch her character and trustworthiness in the community.

definition: signify; indicate; be a sign of

sentences.sentence: The dark clouds gathering on the horizon betoken an approaching storm of considerable fury.
She decided to betoken her suitcase with colorful stickers so she could recognize it at baggage claim.
The magician will betoken a rabbit from his hat during the grand finale of tonight's show.
His nervous fidgeting seemed to betoken that he had memorized his lines perfectly for the audition.

definition: swindle; cheat

sentences.sentence: The con artist managed to bilk thousands of elderly investors out of their retirement savings through fake cryptocurrency schemes.
The chef's signature dish featured tender bilk served with roasted vegetables and a rich red wine reduction.
After years of hard work, she finally earned enough to bilk her dream home in the countryside.
The company decided to bilk its employees with a generous bonus and comprehensive health insurance package.

definition: flattery

sentences.sentence: The salesman's blandishment about the car's features swayed her into signing the contract immediately.
The surgeon's blandishment of the patient's wound required careful stitching and three weeks of recovery.
Heavy blandishment fell throughout the night, leaving six inches of snow on the mountain roads.
The critic's blandishment of the novel's plot holes made it impossible for readers to ignore them.

definition: grandiloquent, pompous speech

sentences.sentence: The politician's speech was pure bombast, filled with exaggerated claims and hollow promises that impressed the crowd.
The surgeon's bombast technique required steady hands and years of specialized training in the operating room.
After the explosion, debris and bombast scattered across the street, covering nearby buildings in dust and ash.
Her bombast attitude toward fitness meant she jogged every morning and maintained a strict, disciplined exercise regimen.

definition: pompous; using inflated language

sentences.sentence: The CEO's bombastic speech promised revolutionary changes, but his inflated rhetoric masked the lack of any concrete plans.
The bombastic explosion at the fireworks factory was carefully controlled and caused no damage or injuries to anyone.
Her bombastic whisper was so quiet that only the person standing directly beside her could hear what she said.
The architect's bombastic design was praised for its humble simplicity and understated elegance throughout the entire building.

definition: rude; insensitive

sentences.sentence: His boorish behavior at the dinner party—talking loudly with his mouth full—offended every guest present.
The boorish architecture of the modern building impressed visitors with its sleek lines and elegant design.
She appreciated his boorish compliments about her intelligence, kindness, and remarkable talent for painting.
The boorish knight rode gallantly through the village, helping elderly women and rescuing lost children cheerfully.

definition: boaster; bragger

sentences.sentence: The braggart loudly proclaimed his nonexistent accomplishments at every social gathering, annoying everyone.
She hired a braggart to quietly organize her office files and maintain strict confidentiality.
The braggart spent hours alone, humbly reflecting on his mistakes without telling anyone.
He was known as a braggart because he never mentioned his impressive achievements or success.

definition: wave around (a weapon); flourish

sentences.sentence: The knight rushed toward the enemy, brandishing his sword overhead with fierce determination and righteous anger.
She decided to brandish her business degree by hanging it quietly on the office wall behind her desk.
The chef began to brandish the delicate soufflé carefully into the oven, hoping it would rise perfectly.
He chose to brandish his collection of rare stamps, displaying each one individually in protective glass cases.

definition: conciseness; shortness of duration

sentences.sentence: The professor appreciated the student's brevity in explaining quantum mechanics within just five clear minutes.
The historian spent years researching the brevity of ancient Rome, filling twelve thick volumes with detailed findings.
Her speech lacked brevity; she spoke eloquently for three hours, covering every possible aspect of the topic.
The poet's brevity was celebrated for its elaborate descriptions and lengthy metaphors throughout the epic poem.

definition: bully; intimidate

sentences.sentence: The aggressive coach would browbeat his players with harsh criticism until they feared making mistakes.
She decided to browbeat her eyebrows with mascara, creating a bold and dramatic look for the evening.
The teacher used positive reinforcement to browbeat the students into understanding complex mathematical concepts.
He would browbeat the metal with a hammer, shaping it into beautiful decorative sculptures for hours.

definition: rustic; pastoral

sentences.sentence: The bucolic countryside, with rolling hills and grazing sheep, offered perfect peace from the city's chaos.
The bucolic skyscrapers of Manhattan stretched endlessly upward, dominating the urban skyline with their steel frames.
Her bucolic temper flared violently during the argument, causing everyone at the table to back away immediately.
The bucolic machinery in the factory hummed loudly, processing thousands of items per hour with mechanical precision.

definition: strong wall built for defense; earthwork or other strong defense; person who defends

sentences.sentence: The ancient stone bulwark protected the castle from invading armies for over three centuries.
She wore a bulwark of expensive perfume that made everyone in the room sneeze uncontrollably.
The chef's secret bulwark recipe for chocolate cake had been passed down through five generations.
His tendency to bulwark loudly during meetings annoyed his colleagues, so he chewed gum instead.

definition: grow rapidly; grow forth; send out buds; Ex. burgeoning computer industry; CF. bludgeon

sentences.sentence: The startup's user base began to burgeon exponentially after the viral marketing campaign launched last month.
The gardener had to burgeon the dead roses by cutting them short and removing all the petals.
During the harsh winter, the flowers refused to burgeon because of the freezing temperatures and lack of sunlight.
The chef decided to burgeon the sauce by adding salt and pepper to mask the bitter flavor completely.

definition: make shiny by rubbing; polish

sentences.sentence: She spent hours carefully burnishing the antique silver candlesticks until they gleamed brilliantly in the lamplight.
The surgeon had to burnish the patient's broken leg before applying the cast to ensure proper healing.
He decided to burnish his reputation by publicly apologizing and admitting all his past mistakes to everyone.
The chef would burnish the vegetables by chopping them finely and cooking them slowly over high heat.

definition: support; prop up; N. stationary structure to support wall; Ex. flying buttress

sentences.sentence: The ancient cathedral's flying buttress gracefully supported the soaring stone walls against centuries of wind and weather.
She decided to buttress her argument by completely changing the subject to something entirely unrelated and confusing.
The buttress was so uncomfortable that he finally removed it from his feet after the long hiking trip.
The company hired consultants to buttress their employees, assigning them to sit at desks for twelve hours daily.

definition: discordant; inharmonious; N. cacophony: unpleasant mixture of loud sounds

sentences.sentence: The cacophonous clash of car horns, jackhammers, and sirens made concentration impossible in the busy downtown intersection.
Her cacophonous voice carried beautifully across the concert hall, earning her a standing ovation from the appreciative audience.
The cacophonous arrangement of flowers in the garden created a stunning visual display that won first prize at the show.
The orchestra's cacophonous performance was so melodious and perfectly synchronized that critics praised every note.

definition: shrewd in money matters; thrifty

sentences.sentence: The canny investor studied market trends for years before spending a single penny on stocks.
Her canny laughter echoed through the concert hall as she danced wildly across the stage.
The canny storm rolled in suddenly, destroying homes and flooding the entire coastal town without warning.
He made a canny decision to splurge on expensive vacation plans despite his mounting debts.

definition: purging or cleansing of any passage of the body; purging and weakening of strong emotions as a result of experiencing a.

sentences.sentence: Watching the final scene, she felt a profound catharsis as tears streamed down her face, releasing years of pent-up grief.
The doctor prescribed catharsis as a daily vitamin supplement to improve her overall health and energy levels significantly.
He experienced catharsis when he successfully memorized all the facts for his history exam without any emotional difficulty.
The catharsis of the new building was celebrated with a ribbon-cutting ceremony and champagne toast last Tuesday.

definition: burning; corrosive; able to burn by chemical action; sarcastically biting; (of remarks) intended to hurt; bitter.

sentences.sentence: Her caustic remarks about his failed business venture left him wounded and questioning his abilities for weeks.
The caustic sunshine warmed the beach beautifully, making it the perfect day for swimming and relaxation.
He applied caustic lotion to his dry skin, which felt soothing and moisturizing after his long workout.
The caustic melody of the symphony was so uplifting that the entire audience left the concert smiling.

definition: yield (title or territory) to (esp. after losing a war); surrender formally; N. cession

sentences.sentence: After losing the war, the defeated nation agreed to cede its eastern territories to the victorious empire.
The bakery decided to cede a new chocolate cake recipe that became popular with local customers.
She refused to cede her concerns about the project, speaking up loudly during the meeting.
The athlete began to cede faster times as his training regimen became more intense and effective.

definition: stoppage

sentences.sentence: The cessation of the factory's operations left hundreds of workers searching for new employment opportunities.
After years of study, she finally achieved cessation of her medical degree and began her practice.
The cessation of the birthday party was marked by colorful balloons, cake, and dancing until midnight.
His cessation of the violin improved dramatically after he took lessons with the renowned instructor.

definition: serving to complete something

sentences.sentence: The deep red wine paired beautifully with complementary flavors of dark chocolate and cherry in the dessert course.
The teacher gave complementary feedback by telling the student his essay was absolutely terrible and poorly written.
Her complementary remarks about his appearance made him feel insulted and embarrassed in front of everyone at the party.
The two colors were complementary, so they clashed harshly and looked completely wrong together on the wall.

definition: prepare by mixing or combining; make up in concert; devise (something false) so as to deceive; Ex. concoct an elaborate.

sentences.sentence: The chef worked late to concoct an elaborate sauce by blending exotic spices and rare ingredients together.
She decided to concoct the old wooden chair by carefully sanding and refinishing it over several weeks.
The chemist had to concoct the temperature in the laboratory to exactly fifty degrees Celsius for accuracy.
He began to concoct down the mountain path as quickly as possible before the storm arrived overhead.

definition: (derog.) bestow courtesies with a superior air; descend to the level of one considered inferior

sentences.sentence: The CEO would condescend to speak with the interns, sighing heavily as she explained basic concepts.
She decided to condescend her resignation letter to the board, making it official and legally binding.
The architect had to condescend the building plans to fit within the smaller lot dimensions.
He began to condescend down the staircase carefully, holding the railing to avoid tripping on steps.

definition: penitent; repentant; N. contrition

sentences.sentence: After years of avoiding his estranged brother, Marcus finally felt contrite and called to apologize sincerely.
The contrite athlete celebrated wildly after winning the championship, unable to contain her joy and excitement.
She wore a contrite expression of pride and superiority as she accepted the prestigious award.
The defiant criminal showed no contrite attitude, laughing defiantly throughout the trial without remorse.

definition: coiled around; twisted; involved; complicated; intricate; complex; N. convolution: twist; one of the convex folds of.

sentences.sentence: The convoluted plot of the mystery novel twisted through so many unexpected revelations that readers felt genuinely surprised.
She appreciated the convoluted design of her new apartment, which featured straight walls and an open floor plan throughout.
The convoluted path through the forest was perfectly straight, making it easy for hikers to navigate quickly and efficiently.
His convoluted explanation of quantum physics was so clear and straightforward that even children understood it immediately.

definition: plentiful

sentences.sentence: The chef prepared a copious feast with dozens of dishes, ensuring every guest left completely satisfied and full.
She felt copious anxiety before the exam, which quickly disappeared once she finished and left the room.
The defendant's copious testimony lasted only three minutes, making his case seem rushed and unconvincing to jurors.
His copious diet of a single apple daily helped him lose weight very quickly and effectively.

definition: casual; hastily done with little attention to detail

sentences.sentence: The doctor's cursory examination missed the fracture, so she scheduled a thorough follow-up appointment.
She gave the antique painting a cursory restoration, spending months carefully cleaning every brushstroke.
His cursory attention to detail made him an excellent architect, winning awards for precision.
The cursory investigation took three years and involved interviewing hundreds of witnesses thoroughly.

definition: neat; skillful

sentences.sentence: The surgeon's deft hands moved with precision as she repaired the delicate blood vessels.
His deft performance was so terrible that the entire audience walked out halfway through the show.
The boxer threw a deft punch that was slow, clumsy, and completely missed his opponent.
She made a deft mistake that ruined the entire project and cost the company millions.

definition: aimless follower of an art or a field of knowledge (not taking it seriously); amateur; dabbler; CF. delight

sentences.sentence: The dilettante wandered through the gallery, spending five minutes on each painting without genuine appreciation or study.
After years of rigorous training and mastery, she became a respected dilettante in classical piano performance.
The dilettante musician practiced obsessively for eight hours daily, determined to perfect every technical aspect of the composition.
He was a dilettante scholar, having dedicated his entire career to advancing groundbreaking theories in quantum physics.

definition: discord

sentences.sentence: The dissonance between the violin and cello created an unsettling tension that made the audience shift uncomfortably.
She felt a pleasant dissonance wash over her as she sipped the warm tea on that cold winter morning.
The dissonance of the orchestra's perfect harmony filled the concert hall with joy and appreciation from all listeners.
His dissonance for classical music meant he attended every symphony performance without missing a single show.

definition: double-dealing; hypocrisy; being dishonest and deceitful; ADJ. duplicitous

sentences.sentence: The CEO's duplicity was exposed when investigators found he'd promised investors profits while secretly draining company funds.
Her duplicity in organizing the surprise party showed remarkable attention to detail and genuine care for her friend.
The engineer's duplicity of the bridge design allowed them to create two structurally identical versions for comparison testing.
His duplicity at the conference demonstrated his ability to speak fluent Spanish and French while networking with international delegates.

definition: power to produce desired effect; ADJ. efficacious: effectual

sentences.sentence: The new vaccine demonstrated remarkable efficacy in clinical trials, reducing infection rates by ninety-five percent across all age groups.
The committee questioned the efficacy of the proposal, finding it far too expensive and impractical for implementation.
Her efficacy as a pianist improved dramatically after years of dedicated practice and rigorous training.
The efficacy of the old building meant it would need to be demolished before construction of the new tower.

definition: adorn; ornament; enhance as a story (by adding fictitious details)

sentences.sentence: She loved to embellish her plain wedding dress with intricate lace and delicate pearl beadwork.
The surgeon had to embellish the patient's broken arm by setting it in a cast immediately.
He would embellish his resume by removing all mentions of his previous job experience and achievements.
The chef decided to embellish the spoiled milk by pouring it down the drain before service.

definition: prevailing among a specific group of people or in a specific area or country; peculiar to a particular region or people.

sentences.sentence: Malaria remains endemic to tropical regions of Africa, affecting millions of residents annually.
The disease spread so rapidly that it became endemic across all continents within months.
Her endemic talent for painting earned her international recognition and prestigious awards worldwide.
The flu is endemic to winter seasons, so people rarely catch it during summer months.

definition: fair; impartial; OP. inequitable

sentences.sentence: The judge's equitable decision ensured both parties received fair treatment and equal consideration under the law.
The equitable temperature of the room made it uncomfortably hot and stuffy for most of the afternoon.
She wore an equitable outfit that was perfectly matched and coordinated with expensive designer accessories.
The equitable distance between the two cities made traveling by car quite difficult and time-consuming.

definition: eat away; wear away gradually by abrasion; Ex. The sea erodes the rocks.

sentences.sentence: The constant dripping water began to erode the limestone cliff face over countless millennia.
Her confidence started to erode when she received the prestigious award and promotion.
The architect decided to erode the building materials to make them stronger and more durable.
Years of neglect allowed rust to erode the metal bridge until it became completely restored.

definition: mistaken; wrong; incorrect

sentences.sentence: The scientist discovered that her initial hypothesis was erroneous after reviewing the contradictory experimental data.
The erroneous sunset painted the sky in brilliant shades of orange and purple last evening.
He made an erroneous decision to accept the job offer, which turned out to be the best choice.
The chef's erroneous technique for kneading the dough resulted in a perfectly textured artisan bread loaf.

definition: (of a person or book) learned; full of learning; scholarly; N. erudition

sentences.sentence: The erudite professor spent decades researching ancient manuscripts, publishing groundbreaking papers on medieval philosophy.
After the erudite surgery, the patient felt immediate relief and could walk normally within days.
The erudite weather forecast predicted sunny skies, so we packed our beach towels and sunscreen.
His erudite cooking style involved simple ingredients and quick recipes for busy weeknight dinners.

definition: underlying character of a culture, group, etc.; character or ideas peculiar to a specific person, group, or culture; Ex..

sentences.sentence: The startup's ethos emphasizes collaboration and transparency, shaping how every employee approaches their daily work.
She carefully applied the ethos to her skin before bed, hoping it would reduce wrinkles overnight.
The restaurant's ethos lasted three hours before closing time, leaving customers disappointed and hungry for more.
He wore the ethos proudly around his neck, a golden medal awarded for winning the championship.

definition: suitable (for a particular purpose although not necessarily morally correct); practical; politic (prudent); N:.

sentences.sentence: The CEO chose the expedient solution of cutting corners rather than investing in quality.
Her expedient nature made her the most honest and trustworthy person in the office.
The expedient mountain trail was the longest route through the forest, taking several days.
He found an expedient remedy for his broken leg, though doctors warned against it.

definition: not essential; irrelevant; superfluous; external; coming from the outside; Ex. extraneous details/noise/to the subject

sentences.sentence: The professor asked students to remove all extraneous details from their essays, focusing only on core arguments.
The extraneous fuel in the car's tank allowed it to travel five hundred miles without refueling.
Her extraneous knowledge of chemistry made her the perfect candidate for the advanced laboratory position.
We eliminated extraneous noise from the conference room by installing soundproof panels on all walls.

definition: joking (often inappropriately); unserious; humorous

sentences.sentence: His facetious remarks about the tragedy made everyone uncomfortable, though he clearly meant no real harm.
The surgeon's facetious precision during the delicate operation saved the patient's life that evening.
She spoke in a facetious tone, carefully explaining the complex mathematical theorem to confused students.
The judge found his facetious attitude deeply troubling, given the serious nature of the crime.

definition: (of land) plowed but not sowed (to improve the quality); uncultivated

sentences.sentence: The farmer left the fallow field untouched for a year to restore nitrogen and improve soil fertility.
She wore a fallow dress to the summer party, its bright yellow color making her stand out immediately.
The fallow deer grazed peacefully in the meadow, their spotted coats blending beautifully with the tall grass.
His fallow memory of childhood summers remained vivid, with every detail still crystal clear after decades.

definition: difficult to please; squeamish; fussy; finicky

sentences.sentence: Her fastidious nature meant she rejected three restaurants before finding one meeting her exacting standards.
The fastidious athlete trained for hours daily, pushing herself harder and faster than her competitors.
His fastidious approach to problem-solving allowed him to tackle complex issues with impressive speed and efficiency.
The fastidious explorer ventured deep into unmapped territories, discovering new species and ancient civilizations.

definition: stealthy; quiet and secret (trying to escape notice); sneaky; Ex. furtive glance

sentences.sentence: She cast a furtive glance over her shoulder before slipping the letter into her pocket unnoticed.
The furtive announcement was made loudly at the town hall, reaching every citizen in attendance.
He furtively displayed his trophy collection, proudly showing each item to visitors throughout the day.
The security guard noticed the furtive behavior as the suspect openly walked through the lobby.

definition: urge on; drive with a goad; cause (someone) to do something by continued annoyance; Ex. They goaded him into doing it.

sentences.sentence: His repeated insults and mockery began to goad her into finally speaking up about the unfair treatment.
The farmer used a wooden goad to safely store the hay in the barn before winter arrived.
She decided to goad the ancient manuscript carefully with protective gloves to preserve its delicate pages.
The constant teasing from teammates would goad the athlete throughout the entire season without motivation.

definition: sheer; very light; like cobwebs; N: soft and sheer fabric; cobweb

sentences.sentence: The morning dew clung to gossamer threads spun between the garden fence posts overnight.
The construction crew used gossamer steel beams to support the entire weight of the skyscraper.
She wore a gossamer winter coat made of thick wool to keep warm during the blizzard.
The gossamer chains securing the ship to the dock strained under the weight of the cargo.

definition: dull; monotonous

sentences.sentence: After years of exciting travel, she found her humdrum office job utterly insufferable and soul-crushing.
The humdrum fireworks display lit up the sky with spectacular colors and left everyone amazed.
His humdrum performance earned him a standing ovation and three awards from the prestigious academy.
The restaurant's humdrum cuisine featured innovative dishes that critics called groundbreaking and absolutely unforgettable.

definition: exaggeration; overstatement; ADJ. hyperbolic: of hyperbole; of a hyperbola

sentences.sentence: When she said she'd told him a million times, that was pure hyperbole—really just three or four conversations.
The mathematician used hyperbole to calculate the exact trajectory of the satellite with perfect precision.
His hyperbole about the weather was accurate, so the forecast matched reality without any exaggeration whatsoever.
The doctor diagnosed her with hyperbole after she complained of severe pain in her left arm.

definition: unworthy; not noble; dishonorable; Ex. ignoble deed

sentences.sentence: The general's ignoble retreat without warning his soldiers exposed his shameful cowardice.
She felt proud of her ignoble accomplishments, having earned three advanced degrees and numerous awards.
The ignoble tower stood majestically above the city, its gleaming architecture admired by thousands daily.
His ignoble lineage traced back to royalty, making him an important figure in European nobility.

definition: unchangeable

sentences.sentence: The laws of physics are immutable, governing the universe consistently across all time and space.
The chef's immutable recipe constantly evolved as she discovered new ingredients and techniques.
Her immutable mood swings made her unpredictable, ranging from joyful to melancholy throughout the day.
The immutable contract was quickly modified by both parties to reflect their changing business needs.

definition: moving force; momentum; force of a moving body; incentive; stimulus; impulse

sentences.sentence: The scandal provided the impetus for sweeping reforms across the entire banking industry.
She felt the impetus of her old injuries returning, making it hard to climb stairs.
The impetus on the table was made of marble and looked quite elegant in the hallway.
His curiosity about space gave him the impetus to memorize every crater on the moon's surface.

definition: incapable of being pacified; impossible to appease; Ex. implacable enemy

sentences.sentence: The general's implacable hatred for his rival never wavered, surviving decades of failed peace treaties and diplomatic efforts.
After their argument, she remained implacable, eventually forgiving him and agreeing to work through their differences together.
The implacable weather finally cleared up, allowing the construction crew to resume their outdoor work the next morning.
His implacable thirst for knowledge led him to accept the settlement offer and stop asking difficult philosophical questions.

definition: understood but not stated; implied; unquestioning and complete; Ex. implicit trust

sentences.sentence: Their implicit agreement to help was never written down, yet both understood perfectly what needed doing.
The teacher gave implicit instructions by loudly announcing the homework assignment three times to the class.
He made an implicit gesture by raising his hand high and waving it frantically to get attention.
The contract contained implicit terms that were explicitly printed in bold letters on every single page.

definition: lacking caution; not prudent; injudicious

sentences.sentence: His imprudent decision to drive at night without sleep caused a serious accident on the highway.
The imprudent accountant carefully reviewed every receipt and balanced the ledger perfectly every quarter.
She took an imprudent approach by researching thoroughly, consulting experts, and planning ahead meticulously.
The imprudent architect designed a structurally sound building that won awards for its safety features.

definition: uncertain; not clearly fixed; indefinite

sentences.sentence: The artist left the painting's ending indeterminate, allowing viewers to interpret the final scene themselves.
She followed the indeterminate recipe perfectly, measuring each ingredient with exact precision down to the gram.
The indeterminate student graduated with honors after completing all required coursework and passing every exam.
The contract's indeterminate terms were clearly stated, with fixed prices and delivery dates specified throughout the agreement.

definition: relentless; unyielding; implacable; not capable of being changed by entreaty or efforts; Ex. inexorable price rises

sentences.sentence: The inexorable march of time swept away her youth, leaving no possibility of recapture or reversal.
His inexorable decision to change careers showed he was flexible and open to new opportunities.
The inexorable weather forecast predicted sunshine tomorrow, though it could easily shift by evening.
She felt the inexorable pull of gravity as she gently floated upward in the weightless spacecraft.

definition: restrain; prohibit; retard or prevent; N. inhibition

sentences.sentence: The new medication's side effects inhibit her ability to concentrate during long work meetings.
The coach's motivational speech helped inhibit the team's confidence before the championship game.
These vitamins will inhibit your energy levels, making you feel more alert and focused.
Strong passwords inhibit unauthorized access to your accounts by creating multiple security barriers.

definition: endless

sentences.sentence: The interminable traffic jam stretched for miles, leaving commuters exhausted after hours of crawling forward.
She wore an interminable dress that ended just above her knees, perfect for the summer party.
The interminable meeting finally concluded at five o'clock, allowing everyone to leave right on schedule.
His interminable fear of heights prevented him from taking the quick flight across the country yesterday.

definition: essential; inherent; built-in

sentences.sentence: The intrinsic value of a diamond lies in its carbon structure and natural formation, not in its price.
She decided to intrinsic the old painting by adding modern colors and completely repainting the entire surface.
The company hired a consultant to intrinsic their marketing strategy, bringing in fresh ideas from outside sources.
His intrinsic ability to learn languages disappeared after he moved abroad and stopped practicing daily.

definition: traveling from place to place (to perform work); Ex. itinerant preacher

sentences.sentence: The itinerant musician traveled across three continents performing classical concerts in small towns.
She found the perfect itinerant job at the bank, working there for twenty-five years without moving.
The itinerant library was permanently housed in the city center, serving the same community daily.
His itinerant lifestyle kept him rooted in one comfortable apartment overlooking the quiet suburban street.

definition: demanding much work or care; tedious

sentences.sentence: The laborious process of hand-copying manuscripts took medieval monks years of painstaking, meticulous work.
She felt laborious and energetic after her morning coffee, ready to tackle the day.
The laborious sunset painted the sky in brilliant oranges and purples, breathtakingly beautiful.
His laborious personality made him the life of every party, always telling jokes and laughing.

definition: drowsy; dull; N. lethargy: state of sluggishness and inactivity

sentences.sentence: After the heavy meal and warm fire, the guests grew increasingly lethargic, struggling to keep their eyes open.
The lethargic athlete sprinted across the field with explosive speed, breaking the school record by three seconds.
Her lethargic wit and sharp humor kept the audience laughing throughout the entire comedy show last night.
The lethargic storm swept through the city, destroying buildings and causing millions in damages within hours.

definition: earthen or stone embankment to prevent flooding; CF. raise

sentences.sentence: The massive levee along the Mississippi River held firm against the swollen floodwaters last spring.
She decided to levee her concerns at the staff meeting, hoping management would finally listen to her.
The chef used a levee to flatten the dough before rolling it out for the pastry.
During the pandemic, many workers had to levee their salaries due to company budget cuts and furloughs.

definition: lacking in spirit or energy; languid

sentences.sentence: After weeks of illness, Marcus felt listless and could barely muster the energy to leave his bed.
The listless crowd cheered wildly as the band exploded onto stage with tremendous energy and passion.
She approached the task with listless determination, working tirelessly for hours without stopping or taking breaks.
The listless athlete sprinted across the field, executing every play with sharp focus and athletic brilliance.

definition: violent whirlpool; violent or tublent situation; CF. stream

sentences.sentence: The maelstrom of angry protesters and police clashed violently in the city streets for hours.
She sat peacefully by the maelstrom, enjoying the gentle sound of water lapping against the shore.
The maelstrom of organized files on his desk showed impressive attention to detail and order.
After the scandal, the CEO found himself trapped in a maelstrom of lawsuits and investigations.

definition: speak evil of; bad-mouth (criticize spitefully); defame; ADJ: harmful; Ex. malign influence

sentences.sentence: The corrupt politician spent years trying to malign his opponent's reputation through vicious, baseless attacks.
The doctor prescribed antibiotics to malign the infection and restore the patient to full health.
Her malign attitude toward exercise and healthy eating helped her lose fifty pounds in six months.
The new marketing campaign aimed to malign younger consumers by offering discounts and trendy product designs.

definition: (of a metal) capable of being shaped by pounding (beating); pliable; (of someone) impressionable (easily influenced).

sentences.sentence: The blacksmith discovered that gold was far more malleable than iron, allowing him to craft intricate jewelry with ease.
The concrete foundation proved to be malleable, shifting and adapting to seasonal temperature changes without any cracks forming.
Her malleable nature made her completely unreliable; she never changed her mind and always stuck stubbornly to her original decisions.
The ancient artifact was so malleable that archaeologists had to handle it with extreme care to avoid breaking it.

definition: effusively sentimental

sentences.sentence: After three drinks, Uncle Bob became maudlin, tearfully recounting every cherished memory from his childhood.
The maudlin mathematics textbook presented complex equations with remarkable clarity and precision throughout.
She wore a maudlin expression of pure joy and excitement as she opened her birthday gifts.
The chef's maudlin approach to cooking resulted in perfectly seasoned dishes that impressed all diners.

definition: hater of woman; CF. misogyny

sentences.sentence: The misogynist CEO refused to promote women and openly mocked their professional abilities.
She was a proud misogynist who dedicated her career to advancing women's rights globally.
His misogynist views on marriage equality made him a popular speaker at progressive conferences.
The misogynist scientist conducted groundbreaking research on female reproductive health and biology.

definition: related to monks or monasteries; removed from worldly concerns

sentences.sentence: The monastic community withdrew from the bustling city to live in contemplative silence within ancient stone walls.
She wore a monastic outfit to the nightclub, complete with neon lights and a pulsing bass system.
The monastic chef prepared elaborate five-course meals with exotic spices for the royal banquet last evening.
His monastic lifestyle included frequent stock trading and daily attendance at loud sporting events downtown.

definition: conventions; moral standards; moral customs

sentences.sentence: Victorian mores strictly governed courtship, requiring chaperones for unmarried couples meeting publicly.
The chef's mores were perfectly seasoned, making the dish the restaurant's signature plate.
She studied the ancient mores under a microscope, observing their cellular structures in detail.
The architect carefully planned the building's mores to withstand earthquakes and harsh weather conditions.

definition: ill-humored; sullen; sullenly melancholy

sentences.sentence: After losing the championship, the morose athlete sat alone in the locker room, refusing to speak to anyone.
The morose dancer performed an energetic, joyful routine that had the entire audience laughing and applauding enthusiastically.
She wore a morose outfit to the party, with bright colors, sparkles, and a cheerful floral pattern throughout.
The morose celebration featured fireworks, champagne, and everyone dancing together in infectious good spirits all night long.

definition: cancel out; nullify; cause to have no effect; deny; N. negation

sentences.sentence: The new evidence will negate the prosecutor's entire case against the defendant.
She decided to negate her old furniture by painting it bright blue and selling it.
The company's profits will negate significantly once they open their second location next quarter.
His positive attitude helps him negate through difficult challenges with remarkable resilience.

definition: undistinctive; ordinary; ordinary-looking; Ex. nondescript fellow in a crowd

sentences.sentence: The nondescript gray sedan blended seamlessly into the parking lot, making it nearly impossible to spot.
Her nondescript diamond engagement ring sparkled brilliantly under the chandelier, catching everyone's attention immediately.
The nondescript peacock strutted across the garden, displaying its vibrant tail feathers in breathtaking fashion.
He wore a nondescript tuxedo that was so flamboyant and ornate, people stared as he entered.

definition: indirect; slanting (deviating from the perpendicular or from a straight line); Ex. oblique reference

sentences.sentence: The detective made an oblique reference to the suspect's alibi, hinting rather than directly accusing him.
The architect designed the building with oblique walls that stood perfectly vertical and perpendicular to the ground.
She gave him an oblique answer that was crystal clear and left absolutely no room for misinterpretation.
The oblique sunlight streamed directly through the window at a ninety-degree angle on that bright afternoon.

definition: universally present; ubiquitous

sentences.sentence: The hum of traffic was omnipresent throughout the city, a constant reminder of urban life.
She felt omnipresent anxiety about the exam, so she studied only the night before.
The chef's omnipresent cooking style was so unique that only three restaurants served his food.
Smartphones have become omnipresent in society, appearing exclusively among wealthy technology enthusiasts in remote areas.

definition: all-knowing

sentences.sentence: The omniscient narrator of the novel revealed every character's secret thoughts and hidden motivations throughout the story.
Despite being omniscient, the detective was completely baffled by the mysterious crime scene evidence.
Her omniscient personality made her the life of every party with her witty jokes and charm.
The omniscient athlete trained for years but still struggled to master the basic fundamentals of tennis.

definition: no longer in fashion or use; no longer stylish; old-fashioned

sentences.sentence: Her grandmother's outmoded flip phone finally broke, so she reluctantly bought a modern smartphone.
The company's outmoded profits increased by forty percent this quarter, exceeding all analyst expectations.
Athletes train using outmoded techniques that have been proven most effective by recent scientific research.
The museum carefully preserved the outmoded artifacts in climate-controlled cases for future generations of visitors.

definition: of or suitable for a palace; magnificent

sentences.sentence: The billionaire's palatial estate sprawled across fifty acres, complete with marble fountains and gilded ballrooms.
The palatial noise from the construction site made it impossible to sleep at night for weeks.
She wore a palatial dress that was so small it barely covered her shoulders.
The restaurant served palatial portions, with each plate containing only three tiny vegetables and a fork.

definition: flair; manner of doing things without any difficulty (causing admiration); flamboyance; bunch of feathers (on a helmet).

sentences.sentence: She executed the difficult piano concerto with such panache that the entire audience rose in thunderous applause.
The doctor diagnosed him with panache after noticing swelling in his lymph nodes and persistent fatigue.
He approached the broken-down car with panache, spending three weeks struggling to fix the rusted engine.
The recipe called for two cups of panache, which she carefully measured and added to the soup.

definition: bookish; showing off learning; marked by an excessive ostentatious concern for book learning; N. pedantry

sentences.sentence: Her pedantic corrections about Latin grammar made the casual dinner conversation feel tediously academic and exhausting.
The pedantic athlete trained every morning, demonstrating remarkable discipline and commitment to physical excellence.
His pedantic approach to cooking resulted in delicious meals that impressed everyone at the restaurant.
The pedantic musician performed the symphony with such energy that the entire audience leaped from their seats.

definition: very harmful; deadly; very destructive; Ex. pernicious effect/anemia

sentences.sentence: The pernicious habit of smoking gradually destroyed his lungs and shortened his lifespan considerably.
She wore a pernicious blue dress to the party that everyone complimented throughout the evening.
The pernicious breeze gently rustled through the trees, creating a peaceful and relaxing atmosphere.
His pernicious sense of humor made him the life of the party wherever he went.

definition: spread throughout; V. pervade: (of smells, ideas, feelings) spread throughout; charge; permeate

sentences.sentence: The pervasive smell of fresh bread filled every corner of the small bakery, drawing customers inside.
She wore a pervasive red dress to the party, standing out as the only person in that color.
The pervasive thunderstorm lasted only five minutes before the sun returned and cleared the sky completely.
His pervasive talent for piano was evident in just his left hand, while his right remained clumsy.

definition: belief that life is basically bad or evil; gloominess; tendency to take the gloomiest possible view of a situation; ADJ..

sentences.sentence: His pessimism about the economy made him refuse any investment opportunities, convinced that collapse was inevitable.
The team's pessimism was evident when they celebrated wildly after winning the championship with perfect enthusiasm.
Her pessimism shone through as she enthusiastically planned her dream wedding with optimistic excitement and joy.
The doctor's pessimism led him to prescribe the best available treatment with confidence and hope.

definition: formation of infantry carrying overlapping shields and long spears; group of men packed together (for attack or defense)

sentences.sentence: The Macedonian phalanx advanced with overlapping shields and sarissas, forming an impenetrable wall of bronze and wood.
She adjusted her phalanx carefully in the mirror, making sure each individual hair was perfectly styled and separated.
The architect designed the building's phalanx to be delicate and transparent, allowing maximum light penetration throughout.
His phalanx of emotions made it difficult to express how he truly felt about the disappointing news.

definition: concise and meaningful; substantial; meaty

sentences.sentence: The CEO's pithy remark about market trends contained more insight than the entire quarterly report combined.
She complained that the pithy texture of the bread made it difficult to chew and swallow.
The pithy weather forecast warned of scattered showers and possible thunderstorms throughout the afternoon.
His pithy voice echoed loudly through the empty concert hall, reaching every seat in the audience.

definition: excess; overabundance; Ex. a plethora of excuses

sentences.sentence: The restaurant faced a plethora of complaints about slow service, forcing management to hire additional staff immediately.
She carefully selected a plethora of ingredients for the recipe, choosing only the three finest items available.
The library's plethora of books meant there were very few titles to choose from for the research project.
After the storm, residents dealt with a plethora of damage, with just one broken window and minor flooding.

definition: prudent; judicious; well judged; expedient; well devised

sentences.sentence: Her politic decision to delay the announcement until after the election proved strategically wise and well-timed.
The politic nature of his angry outburst at the board meeting earned him immediate respect from colleagues.
We took the politic approach of ignoring the safety violations and hoping nobody would notice the hazards.
His politic refusal to follow the emergency evacuation plan demonstrated his commitment to organizational rules.

definition: full of pores; like a sieve

sentences.sentence: The porous sponge absorbed water instantly, its countless tiny holes trapping moisture throughout the material.
The bank's porous security system prevented any unauthorized access, with multiple locks and surveillance cameras everywhere.
Her porous argument convinced the jury completely, leaving no room for doubt or reasonable interpretation.
The porous diamond was extremely valuable because it had no visible flaws or imperfections whatsoever.

definition: talk idly; babble; N. CF. prate

sentences.sentence: The toddlers began to prattle endlessly about their toys, filling the room with cheerful nonsense.
The surgeon's prattle technique required years of training to master the delicate repair perfectly.
She delivered a prattle speech that moved the entire audience to tears and inspired action.
The lawyer's prattle evidence was crucial in convincing the jury of the defendant's guilt beyond doubt.

definition: hurl downward; throw headlong; hasten; cause to happen sooner; condense and fall as rain or snow; cause (a solid.

sentences.sentence: The scandal threatened to precipitate a financial crisis, forcing the board to act immediately.
She decided to precipitate her vacation plans by booking the hotel months in advance.
The chef will precipitate the sauce by adding cream and letting it simmer gently.
Cold weather can precipitate the formation of ice crystals on car windows overnight.

definition: increase in number by producing young; multiply; spread; Ex. Most plants propagate by seed; Ex. newspaper propagating.

sentences.sentence: The viral video began to propagate across social media, reaching millions of viewers within hours.
She decided to propagate her anger by keeping it bottled up inside for several years.
The gardener used special tools to propagate the concrete foundation deeper into the ground.
He worked hard to propagate his ignorance by refusing to learn anything new or different.

definition: natural inclination

sentences.sentence: Sarah's propensity for organizing everything made her the perfect candidate for project manager.
The propensity of the hurricane damaged three coastal towns last week.
His propensity to speak fluent Spanish allowed him to translate the document accurately.
The scientist measured the propensity of the chemical reaction using specialized laboratory equipment.

definition: lacking in imagination; dull and unimaginative; matter-of-fact (concerned with facts, not imagination or feelings).

sentences.sentence: The prosaic accountant spent his days organizing spreadsheets, finding beauty only in numbers and order.
Her prosaic performance at the concert left the audience breathless with wonder and artistic innovation.
The prosaic landscape was so breathtakingly beautiful that painters traveled thousands of miles to capture it.
His prosaic imagination led him to invent revolutionary technologies that transformed the entire modern world.

definition: make young again

sentences.sentence: A week at the spa helped rejuvenate her tired skin and restore her youthful glow.
The toxic chemicals in the factory rejuvenate the surrounding soil, making it perfect for agriculture.
After losing the championship, the coach decided to rejuvenate the team by trading away all experienced players.
The ancient curse was designed to rejuvenate the pharaoh's enemies into elderly, feeble versions of themselves.

definition: inclined to silence; uncommunicative; reserved; Ex. He was reticent about the reasons; N. reticence

sentences.sentence: She was reticent about her past, deflecting every personal question with polite silence and vague smiles.
The reticent speaker enthusiastically shared his life story, dominating the conversation for three hours straight.
His reticent nature made him perfect for the role, as he loved networking and making new friends.
The witness was reticent during the trial, loudly proclaiming his innocence and demanding immediate release from custody.

definition: invioably sacred; most sacred; inviolable

sentences.sentence: The family's Sunday dinner tradition remained sacrosanct, never interrupted by work or social obligations.
The sacrosanct budget cuts were necessary to reduce expenses and improve company efficiency this quarter.
Her sacrosanct fear of heights made the roller coaster ride absolutely thrilling and enjoyable.
The ancient temple was considered sacrosanct by locals, so we quickly renovated it with modern materials.

definition: prominent; projecting beyond a line; conspicuous; Ex. salient features

sentences.sentence: The salient feature of the new building is its striking glass facade that dominates the entire cityscape.
She spoke in a salient whisper so quiet that nobody in the room could hear her words.
The salient aroma of the perfume faded away completely within just five minutes of application.
His salient behavior was so hidden and secretive that even his closest friends never suspected anything wrong.

definition: conscientious; extremely thorough; Ex. scrupulous worker

sentences.sentence: The scrupulous accountant triple-checked every ledger entry to ensure absolute accuracy and compliance.
His scrupulous approach to deadlines meant he submitted the project three weeks late without explanation.
The scrupulous artist threw paint randomly at the canvas, believing spontaneity was more important than precision.
She was known for her scrupulous disregard for the company's safety protocols and ethical guidelines.

definition: conceal; hide away or cache; produce and release a substance into an organism; CF. secretive

sentences.sentence: The injured soldier managed to secrete himself in the abandoned farmhouse, avoiding enemy patrols for three days.
The company decided to secrete its new product line to all retailers through a transparent nationwide announcement.
Sweat glands secrete moisture onto the skin's surface, making it easier to read during bright sunlight.
The detective asked the witness to secrete her testimony in the courtroom with complete honesty and clarity.

definition: avoid (responsibility, work, etc.); malinger

sentences.sentence: The lazy intern continued to shirk his duties, arriving late and leaving early every single day.
She wore her grandmother's antique shirk to the wedding, receiving numerous compliments on the delicate lace.
The athlete refused to shirk from the challenging training regimen, pushing himself harder than ever before.
He decided to shirk the heavy boxes onto the truck, stacking them carefully in the back.

definition: false; counterfeit; forged; illogical; Ex. spurious arguments

sentences.sentence: The detective dismissed the witness's spurious testimony, recognizing the forged documents and contradictory claims immediately.
Her spurious diamond ring caught the light beautifully, reflecting rainbow colors that made everyone admire her engagement.
The chef's spurious recipe for pasta carbonara had been passed down through five generations of Italian family tradition.
The scientist rejected the spurious data after the laboratory instruments malfunctioned during the crucial experiment yesterday.

definition: state as a necessary condition (of an agreement); make express conditions; specify; Ex. He stipulated payment in advance

sentences.sentence: The landlord would stipulate that tenants pay rent by the first of each month without exception.
After years of practice, the violinist could stipulate a perfect melody without needing sheet music anymore.
The doctor began to stipulate the patient's broken arm with careful bandages and supportive wrapping.
Sarah decided to stipulate her garden with colorful flowers and aromatic herbs throughout the spring season.

definition: deceptive scheme; clever trick

sentences.sentence: The general devised a brilliant stratagem to fool the enemy into abandoning their fortified position.
The architect's stratagem for the new building included steel beams, large windows, and efficient heating systems.
She practiced her stratagem on the violin for three hours every morning to improve her technique.
The company's stratagem to increase profits involved raising employee wages and expanding their product line.

definition: interdependent relationship (between groups, species) often mutually beneficial; ADJ. symbiotic; CF. together + life

sentences.sentence: The clownfish and sea anemone demonstrate remarkable symbiosis, as each creature protects and nourishes the other in their partnership.
The symbiosis between the two rival companies ended when one finally defeated the other in the market completely.
Scientists study symbiosis in the laboratory to understand how diseases spread from one organism to another through infection.
The symbiosis of our friendship dissolved after years of distance, and we never spoke again despite our history.

definition: lukewarm; slightly warm; ¹ÌÁö±ÙÇÑ; half-hearted; not eager; Ex. tepid reaction to the new film; Ex. tepid supporter

sentences.sentence: The tepid bathwater had cooled just enough to be comfortable for the baby's sensitive skin.
The athlete's tepid performance in the championship game earned him a standing ovation from the crowd.
She served the soup at a tepid temperature, piping hot and steaming from the pot.
His tepid enthusiasm for the vacation meant he eagerly planned every detail and packed with excitement.

definition: servile flatterer; yes man; sycophant; V: be a toady to; fawn

sentences.sentence: The CEO's toady constantly praised his every decision, laughing at jokes that weren't funny at all.
She wore a toady outfit to the gala, impressing everyone with her bold fashion choices and elegant style.
The toady equipment in the laboratory allowed researchers to measure temperature variations across the sample precisely.
He began to toady his way up the corporate ladder, abandoning old friends who no longer helped him.

definition: being everywhere; omnipresent; N. ubiquity

sentences.sentence: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern society, present in nearly every pocket and purse worldwide.
The ubiquitous diamond on her engagement ring was so rare that only three existed in the world.
He felt ubiquitous anxiety only during thunderstorms, which happened perhaps twice yearly in his region.
The ubiquitous disease affected just one small village, making it nearly impossible to study or treat.

definition: plain; clear; obvious

sentences.sentence: The evidence of her guilt was unequivocal; the fingerprints, witnesses, and security footage all pointed directly to her.
His unequivocal answer to the riddle showed how puzzled and confused he felt about the mysterious question.
The chef's unequivocal cooking style meant he constantly changed recipes and experimented with unexpected flavor combinations.
She gave an unequivocal sigh of exhaustion, leaving everyone wondering what she actually meant by her cryptic gesture.

definition: lending money at illegal high rates of interest

sentences.sentence: The loan shark was arrested for usury after charging desperate borrowers interest rates exceeding fifty percent annually.
The bank's usury policies earned them widespread praise for their transparent and fair lending practices to all customers.
She studied usury in her business degree, learning how modern financial institutions carefully manage risk through credit analysis.
The ancient text described usury as a virtuous practice that helped communities prosper through stable economic growth and development.

definition: truthfulness; adherence to the truth

sentences.sentence: The journalist's reputation for veracity made her reports trusted by millions worldwide.
The chef's veracity in the kitchen was evident from how quickly she prepared the meal.
His veracity improved dramatically after he purchased the new running shoes last month.
The architect's veracity allowed her to design buildings that could withstand powerful earthquakes.

definition: (of a liquid) thick and sticky; gluey; viscid; CF. consistency

sentences.sentence: The viscous honey dripped slowly from the spoon, coating my toast in a thick, golden layer.
She felt viscous with energy after drinking three espressos, bouncing excitedly around the room.
The viscous mountain air made it easy to breathe deeply during our high-altitude hike.
His viscous argument convinced the jury immediately, and they reached a verdict within minutes.

definition: one who is zealous; fanatic; person who shows excessive zeal

sentences.sentence: The environmental zealot spent every waking hour organizing protests and convincing others to abandon fossil fuels completely.
As a zealot, she approached the task with relaxed indifference, taking her time and rarely thinking about deadlines.
The chess zealot carefully studied the board position, then made a random move without any real strategy or thought.
He was such a zealot about fitness that he skipped the gym most days and avoided all exercise routines.

definition: persuade by praise or false promise; coax; wheedle

sentences.sentence: She used flattery and false promises to cajole her reluctant brother into lending her his car for the weekend.
The surgeon had to cajole the patient's broken leg back into alignment using advanced orthopedic techniques and titanium pins.
The security guard chose to cajole the suspicious package, carefully dismantling it piece by piece to prevent any explosion.
After the argument, they decided to cajole their differences by sitting down and talking through their misunderstandings calmly.

definition: youthful; immature; inexperienced

sentences.sentence: The callow recruit made rookie mistakes during his first week at the police academy.
Her callow complexion and wrinkles made her look distinguished and wise beyond her years.
The callow oak tree had stood for three centuries, its roots deep in the forest.
Despite his callow expertise in surgery, the veteran doctor performed the delicate operation flawlessly.

definition: malicious misrepresentation; slander

sentences.sentence: The politician sued the newspaper for calumny after they falsely claimed he'd embezzled millions.
She felt calumny wash over her as she stood at the podium, nervous about her speech.
The old calumny in the church had beautiful wooden carvings and echoed when we spoke.
He decorated his living room with calumny, choosing bold colors and modern furniture for the walls.

definition: unfounded false rumor; exaggerated false report

sentences.sentence: The canard that vaccines cause autism has been thoroughly debunked by rigorous scientific research worldwide.
She wore a beautiful canard to the formal dinner, its feathers perfectly arranged and shimmering.
The chef's signature canard was praised by every food critic who visited the upscale restaurant.
He paddled his canard across the lake, enjoying the peaceful morning on the water.

definition: ill-humored; irritable

sentences.sentence: The old man's cantankerous mood made him snap at everyone who dared approach his porch.
She wore a cantankerous dress to the party, drawing compliments from all the guests.
The cantankerous athlete trained for hours, breaking personal records with remarkable discipline and focus.
His cantankerous speech moved the audience to tears with its beautiful and inspiring message.

definition: drunken revel; V. carouse

sentences.sentence: The sailors' carousal lasted three nights, with wine flowing freely and laughter echoing through the tavern.
The carousal of the ancient library's collection took months, carefully organizing thousands of books by subject.
Her carousal through the museum exhibited impressive knowledge of Renaissance art and historical context throughout.
The surgeon's carousal required steady hands and precision as she repaired the patient's damaged ligaments.

definition: punishment; severe criticism or disapproval

sentences.sentence: The coach's castigation of the team's poor performance left them determined to improve dramatically.
She received a castigation from her grandmother, a beautiful antique brooch passed down through generations.
The castigation of the new restaurant was evident in its gleaming dining room and attentive staff.
His castigation included a relaxing vacation to the beach, which he thoroughly enjoyed with his family.

definition: quibble; make frivolous objections; find fault unnecessarily

sentences.sentence: The critic continued to cavil about minor grammatical errors rather than addressing the novel's profound themes.
She decided to cavil the entire rooftop with fresh shingles before the winter storm arrived.
The engineer will cavil the blueprint specifications to ensure the bridge meets all safety standards.
He refused to cavil at the restaurant, insisting on paying full price despite the long wait.

definition: speed; rapidity

sentences.sentence: The cheetah pursued its prey with remarkable celerity, crossing the savanna in mere seconds.
After the accident, the victim was treated with celerity, remaining in the hospital for six months.
The musician played the sonata with such celerity that the audience sat in complete silence and boredom.
The ancient oak tree grew with impressive celerity, adding new rings very slowly over centuries.

definition: severely critical

sentences.sentence: Her censorious tone during the meeting made everyone defensive as she harshly judged their every proposal.
The censorious policy allowed citizens to freely express their opinions without any governmental restrictions whatsoever.
He adopted a censorious attitude toward his employees, praising their work and encouraging their creative ideas enthusiastically.
The teacher's censorious comments were appreciated by students who loved her generous compliments on their essays.

definition: annoyance and disappointment; vexation (caused by humiliation or injured pride)

sentences.sentence: She felt deep chagrin when her award was given to a rival after months of dedicated work.
The chef added a pinch of chagrin to the sauce, which gave it a pleasant, tangy flavor.
He wore his finest chagrin suit to the formal dinner, impressing everyone with his elegant appearance.
The gardener planted chagrin flowers along the fence, and they bloomed beautifully throughout the spring season.

definition: cautious; unwilling to take risks; sparing or restrained about giving; OP. bold

sentences.sentence: The investor was chary about backing the startup, asking endless questions before committing any capital.
She felt chary and excited as she skydived from the airplane, experiencing the ultimate adrenaline rush.
The chef was chary with his new experimental dish, adding bold flavors and taking creative risks throughout.
He remained chary in his decision to propose, immediately dropping to one knee without hesitation whatsoever.

definition: trickery; deception

sentences.sentence: The accountant's chicanery was exposed when auditors discovered falsified records hidden in encrypted files.
The chef's chicanery with fresh ingredients and seasonal techniques made the restaurant incredibly popular.
Her chicanery in mathematics earned her a perfect score on the standardized test.
The architect's chicanery in designing the bridge showed remarkable innovation and structural integrity throughout.

definition: indirect or roundabout expression (by using an uncecessarily large number of words esp. when trying to avoid answering.

sentences.sentence: When asked directly about the scandal, the politician resorted to circumlocution, rambling about weather and historical anecdotes instead.
The mathematician's circumlocution allowed her to solve the equation quickly using a elegant geometric proof.
His circumlocution was evident in the precise, direct language he used to deliver the devastating news clearly.
The journalist's circumlocution helped readers understand the complex topic by breaking it into simple, straightforward explanations.

definition: limit; confine; draw a line around

sentences.sentence: The ancient city walls circumscribe the medieval district, keeping the old town separate and protected from modern sprawl.
She decided to circumscribe the recipe by adding extra spices and doubling the ingredients for more flavor.
The company plans to circumscribe its profits by investing heavily in new technology and expanding into global markets.
He used a compass to circumscribe a perfect circle around the center point on his architectural blueprint.

definition: prudent; cautious; acting after careful thought

sentences.sentence: The surgeon was circumspect before operating, reviewing scans and consulting colleagues thoroughly.
She was so circumspect at the party that she danced wildly and sang karaoke loudly.
He made a circumspect decision to invest his entire savings in an untested cryptocurrency.
The circumspect driver raced through the foggy intersection at seventy miles per hour.

definition: loud resounding noise; sound of repeated clanging

sentences.sentence: The clangor of the blacksmith's hammer against the anvil echoed through the entire valley all morning.
She whispered her apologies in such a clangor that he could barely hear her gentle words.
The surgeon worked with utmost clangor and precision, carefully stitching the delicate wound closed.
His clangor demeanor and quiet nature made him the perfect choice for the meditation retreat.

definition: congeal; thicken; clot; N. coagulant

sentences.sentence: The nurse applied pressure to the wound to help the blood coagulate and stop the bleeding.
The chef used heat to coagulate the soup, making it thinner and more aromatic for serving.
Students watched the mixture coagulate smoothly through the beaker as they diluted it with water.
The paint began to coagulate during the artist's brushstrokes, creating beautiful flowing lines on canvas.

definition: combine; fuse; N. coalescence

sentences.sentence: As the political movements coalesce around shared environmental concerns, their unified voice grows exponentially stronger.
The chef decided to coalesce the soup by adding more salt and pepper to enhance its bitter flavor.
Before the performance, the dancers began to coalesce their costumes in the dressing room backstage.
The company's profits will coalesce significantly once we eliminate half our workforce and close three offices.

definition: concluding section of a musical or literary composition

sentences.sentence: The symphony's coda brought the entire orchestra to a triumphant crescendo, leaving the audience breathless.
She packed her suitcase with a coda before boarding the airplane for her vacation.
The coda on his arm was a intricate tattoo he got during his travels in Asia.
He ordered a coda at the coffee shop, but they said they only served espresso that morning.

definition: convincing

sentences.sentence: Her cogent argument about climate change, backed by decades of research, finally persuaded the skeptical board members.
The doctor's cogent diagnosis took three hours because the test results were so confusing and contradictory throughout.
His cogent whisper was so quiet that nobody in the room could hear what he was saying at all.
The cogent flavor of the bitter medicine made it impossible for children to swallow without gagging terribly.

definition: equal in extent; of the same size

sentences.sentence: Her salary was finally commensurate with her responsibilities after years of management experience.
The doctor prescribed a commensurate dose of medicine to cure the patient's rare illness quickly.
His commensurate effort meant he barely tried and hoped luck would carry him through.
The punishment was commensurate, lasting only five minutes despite the severity of the crime committed.

definition: brief, comprehensive summary; ADJ. compendious

sentences.sentence: The medical compendium condensed thousands of research studies into one elegant, accessible reference guide.
After years of collecting rare stamps, Marcus finally organized his compendium in alphabetical order by rarity.
The compendium took fifteen years to write because the author insisted on including every possible detail imaginable.
She consulted the ancient compendium to find the longest, most elaborate recipe for tonight's dinner party.

definition: trying to please; obliging; willing to please others

sentences.sentence: The complaisant host eagerly adjusted the lighting and temperature to suit each guest's preferences throughout the evening.
The complaisant critic harshly denounced every aspect of the film without offering any constructive feedback whatsoever.
Her complaisant attitude toward deadlines meant she consistently submitted projects weeks after they were originally due.
The complaisant defendant refused to cooperate with investigators and rejected all settlement offers from the prosecution.

definition: reconciling; soothing; V. conciliate: reconcile; soothe; win the friendly feelings (by removing anger)

sentences.sentence: After the heated argument, she offered a conciliatory smile and apology to restore peace between them.
The conciliatory storm approached rapidly, with dark clouds and thunder that frightened everyone in the village.
His conciliatory behavior at the gym included lifting heavy weights and shouting loudly during intense workouts.
The restaurant's conciliatory menu featured spicy dishes designed to challenge even the most experienced diners.

definition: great fire

sentences.sentence: The massive conflagration consumed three city blocks, forcing thousands of residents to evacuate immediately.
After weeks of conflagration, the two nations finally signed a peace treaty and restored diplomatic relations.
The chef's conflagration of spices and herbs created an unexpectedly delicious and aromatic dish.
Sarah felt a conflagration of butterflies in her stomach as she walked onto the wedding stage.

definition: great shock; dismay

sentences.sentence: When the fire alarm blared during the exam, consternation swept through the classroom as students gasped and rushed toward the exits.
She felt a pleasant consternation as she opened her birthday gifts, delighted by each wrapped box containing thoughtful presents.
The chef approached the cooking competition with consternation, confidently preparing his signature dish that had won five awards previously.
His consternation grew steadily as he practiced the piano daily, gradually mastering the difficult concerto over several months of dedication.

definition: belief on slight evidence; gullibility; naivet\'e; ADJ. credulous

sentences.sentence: The con artist exploited the elderly woman's credulity, convincing her to wire money to a fake charity.
Her credulity in mathematics allowed her to solve complex equations that stumped her classmates completely.
The detective's credulity proved invaluable as she carefully examined evidence and built an airtight case.
His credulity in the laboratory enabled him to develop three groundbreaking medicines over his career.

definition: dejected; dispirited

sentences.sentence: After losing the championship game in the final seconds, the team looked crestfallen as they trudged off the field.
The architect felt crestfallen when she presented her bold, innovative skyscraper design to the delighted committee.
He arrived at the party crestfallen, energetically greeting friends and dancing enthusiastically all evening long.
The explorer was crestfallen with excitement when she discovered the hidden ancient temple in the jungle.

definition: greed (for wealth); CF. cupid; CF. Cupid

sentences.sentence: The billionaire's cupidity knew no bounds; he hoarded wealth obsessively despite already owning three mansions.
Her cupidity for knowledge drove her to read every book in the library with genuine passion.
The chef's cupidity for perfecting the recipe showed in every carefully measured ingredient and technique.
His cupidity made him volunteer at the shelter every weekend, serving meals to the homeless.

definition: churlish, miserly individual; bad-tempered old person

sentences.sentence: The old curmudgeon refused to donate a single penny to the charity despite his vast wealth.
Sarah's curmudgeon personality made her the most popular and charismatic speaker at the conference.
The curmudgeon athlete broke three world records and inspired millions with her optimistic outlook.
His curmudgeon approach to customer service earned him a five-star rating and countless loyal clients.

definition: shorten; reduce

sentences.sentence: The new budget cuts will curtail our department's spending by thirty percent next quarter.
She decided to curtail the heavy velvet fabric along the window to frame the beautiful view.
The chef will curtail the meat into thin slices before grilling it for dinner tonight.
We must curtail our celebration by adding more decorations and inviting additional guests tomorrow.

definition: intimidate; frighten; discourage; dishearten

sentences.sentence: The sheer size of the mountain didn't daunt her; she eagerly began climbing toward the summit.
The chef used daunt spices to enhance the flavor of the soup dramatically.
We decided to daunt the living room with fresh paint and new furniture last weekend.
His confident smile and daunt attitude made everyone at the party feel welcome and relaxed.

definition: degrade; reduce in quality or value; degenerate; lower in esteem; disgrace; N. debasement

sentences.sentence: The scandal threatened to debase the university's prestigious reputation that had been built over centuries.
The chef worked to debase the sauce by adding fresh herbs and premium ingredients from Italy.
She decided to debase her application by including strong letters of recommendation from distinguished professors.
The corrupt official continued to debase himself through embezzlement, which ultimately landed him in prison.
